title: Tutorial: Accessing and Exploring the Data
text: This tutorial walks through fetching, cleaning, and doing initial exploration of the Utah housing affordability dataset.

title: Tutorial: Accessing and Exploring the Data
text:
Installation
Install the utah-housing package and set your Census API key before running any code cells.
pip install utah-housing
Set your Census API key (obtain a free key at https://api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html):

import os
os.environ["CENSUS_API_KEY"] = "your_census_api_key_here"

title: Tutorial: Accessing and Exploring the Data
text:
Reading in the Data

import pandas as pd
from utah_housing import fetch_all_years, OUTCOME, BASE_PREDICTORS, COMPLEX_PREDICTORS

# Fetch ACS 5-year estimates for Utah census tracts, 2009–2023
df = fetch_all_years(years=range(2009, 2024))
df.head()


# Save to CSV so you can reload without hitting the API again
df.to_csv("data/utah_housing.csv", index=False)


# Reload from CSV (faster than re-fetching)
df = pd.read_csv("data/utah_housing.csv")
df.head()

title: Tutorial: Accessing and Exploring the Data
text:
Initial Exploration

print("Shape:", df.shape)
print("\nOutcome variable:", OUTCOME)
print("Base predictors:", BASE_PREDICTORS)
print("Complex predictors:", COMPLEX_PREDICTORS)


# Check for missing values in key columns
analysis_cols = ["year", "GEOID"] + [OUTCOME] + COMPLEX_PREDICTORS
df[analysis_cols].isnull().sum()

title: Tutorial: Accessing and Exploring the Data
section: Cleaning the Data (not necessary for analysis with package)
text:
Cleaning the Data (not necessary for analysis with package)

# Drop rows missing the outcome variable
df_clean = df.dropna(subset=[OUTCOME]).copy()

# Extract county name from the NAME field for easier filtering
df_clean["county_name"] = df_clean["NAME"].str.extract(r",\s*(.+?)\s+County", expand=False)

print(f"Rows after cleaning: {len(df_clean):,}")
df_clean.head()

title: Tutorial: Accessing and Exploring the Data
text:
Subsetting by County or Year

# Filter to a specific county
salt_lake = df_clean[df_clean["county_name"] == "Salt Lake"]
print(salt_lake.shape)

# Filter to a specific year
df_2023 = df_clean[df_clean["year"] == 2023]
print(df_2023.shape)

title: Tutorial: Accessing and Exploring the Data
text:
Basic Summary Statistics

summary_cols = [OUTCOME] + COMPLEX_PREDICTORS
df_clean[summary_cols].describe().round(2)


The data is now ready for EDA and modeling (see the Technical Report).

title: Utah Housing Affordability Analysis - STAT 386
text:
This is a project website by Sujin Leek and Matthew Conger recording our work with US Census Data regarding Utah housing. We were curious as to what specific factors were affecting the decrease in affordability and availability of single-family homes in Utah.
A streamlit app for seeing exploratory data analysis and plots can be found here.
A link to the Github Repository for the Python package used to complete this analysis here.
See the package at pypi.org here.
This website includes a technical report, documentation, and a tutorial. Navigate using the tabs above.

title: Utah Housing Data Acquisition
text:
ACS 5-year estimates for Utah census tracts, 2009–2023.
Run this notebook once to fetch the data and save it to data/utah_housing.csv. After that, the CSV can be reloaded directly without hitting the Census API again.

title: Utah Housing Data Acquisition
text:
1. Setup

import os
import pandas as pd
from utah_housing import fetch_all_years, OUTCOME, PREDICTORS

title: Utah Housing Data Acquisition
text:
2. Census API Key
Obtain a free key at: https://api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html
Set it below, or store it in a .env file and load with python-dotenv.

# TODO: Replace with your Census API key (do NOT commit this to git)
os.environ["CENSUS_API_KEY"] = "your_census_api_key_here"

title: Utah Housing Data Acquisition
text:
3. Data Acquisition
Fetch ACS 5-year estimates for all Utah census tracts across 2009–2023.

df = fetch_all_years(years=range(2009, 2024))
print(f"Total rows: {len(df):,}")
df.head()

title: Utah Housing Data Acquisition
text:
4. Save Data

os.makedirs("data", exist_ok=True)
df.to_csv("data/utah_housing.csv", index=False)
print("Saved to data/utah_housing.csv")

title: Documentation
text: With this project, we hoped to answer the question: what factors are driving an decrease in single-family-home availability in Utah? We used US Census data, available through (census.gov)[census.gov] to facilitate our analysis.

title: Documentation
text: With this project, we hoped to answer the question: what factors are driving an decrease in single-family-home availability in Utah? We used US Census data, available through (census.gov)[census.gov] to facilitate our analysis.

title: Documentation
text:
Data Source
The data comes from various tables available through the US Census Bureau. One observation corresponds to a single census tract in a single year, meaning there are hundreds of observations per year. We pull for years 2009-2023, but analyze only 2013-2023, that being the year economists have generally decided that the US economy recovered from the 2008 recession.







Table

Units in Structure


B25001
Housing Units


B25003
Tenure (Owner Occupied vs Renter Occupied)


B25008
Total Population in Occupied Housing Units by Tenure


B25119
Median Household Income in the Past 12 Months (in 2024 Inflation-Adjusted Dollars) by Tenure


B25032
Tenure by Units in Structure


B25088
Median Selected Monthly Owner Costs (Dollars) by Mortgage Status


B19013
Median Household Income in the Past 12 Months (in 2024 Inflation-Adjusted Dollars)

title: Documentation
text:
Key Variables
Note that three of the five predictors are derived from other variables; while it seems counterintuitive to pull eight tables and only use five predictors, each table is necessary for the analysis.

Outcome Variable







Variable
Description




[median_owner_costs_with_mortgage]
[median monthly owner costs, with mortgage]





Predictors







Variable
Description




[pct_sf_renter_occupied]
[renter_single_family / single_family_units]


[median_household_income]
[Description]


[owner_renter_income_gap]
[median_income_owner_occupied - median_income_renter_occupied]


[pct_vacant]
[vacant_units / total_units_b25002]


[pop_in_occupied_total]
[Description]

title: Documentation
text:
Python Package: utah-housing
The utah-housing package provides functions to fetch ACS data directly from the Census API and compute derived housing affordability metrics.

Installation
pip install utah-housing

Note: A free Census API key is required. Obtain one at https://api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html and set it as an environment variable:
export CENSUS_API_KEY="your_key_here"



Core Functions

from utah_housing import fetch_year, fetch_all_years, OUTCOME, PREDICTORS, run_model, run_diagnostics

# Fetch ACS 5-year estimates for all years
df = fetch_all_years(years=range(2009, 2024))

# Inspect variable groupings
print("Outcome:", OUTCOME)
print("Predictors:", PREDICTORS)








Function
Description




fetch_year
Fetches ACS estimates for all Utah census tracts across the given year


fetch_all_years(years)
Fetches ACS estimates for all Utah census tracts across the given year range


OUTCOME
String constant naming the outcome variable


PREDICTORS
List of extended predictor variable names


run_model
Builds fixed-effects model as described in technical report


run_diagnostics
Makes sure VIFs and pairwise correlations are acceptable

title: Technical Report
text: A prominent topic over the past decade has been the continued rise in prices of single-family homes in America. Prices of housing contribute to wealth inequality and perpetuate barriers to family formation and upward mobility, which negatively affect many crises that the United States of America currently faces. Our hope with this project was to gain some empirical insight into why housing has become less accessible for young Americans in recent years. Using a fixed-panel regression model, we were able to isolate household income and owner-renter income gap as the two strongest predictors of rising ownership costs.

title: Technical Report
text: A prominent topic over the past decade has been the continued rise in prices of single-family homes in America. Prices of housing contribute to wealth inequality and perpetuate barriers to family formation and upward mobility, which negatively affect many crises that the United States of America currently faces. Our hope with this project was to gain some empirical insight into why housing has become less accessible for young Americans in recent years. Using a fixed-panel regression model, we were able to isolate household income and owner-renter income gap as the two strongest predictors of rising ownership costs.

title: Technical Report
text:
Introduction
Over the past decade, a prominent social and economic issue has been the lack of accessibility to housing. The notion of the American Dream suggests that all citizens should be afforded the privilege of owning a house provided they work hard and manage money accordingly, but in recent years, this dream has become less realistic for many Americans.
While it would have been ideal to look into single-family home purchase accessibility for the entirety of the United States of America, given the constraints we were working with, we decided to focus on data from Utah, found through the US Census.

title: Technical Report
text:
Data Acquisition and Wrangling
Data was obtained through the US Census Bureau’s American Community Survey (2009-2023). We constructed a dataset spanning 772 census tracts across Utah, which includes 393 ZIP codes and 29 counties.
When considering an outcome variable for this analysis, we picked monthly owner costs for households with a mortgage as an indicator of how much it costs a Utahan to purchase and live in a home. Predictor variables were chosen based on easily quantifiable measures, like numbers of units and average income, and measures available through the US Census American Community Survey.
We decided to pull these eight tables, at a granularity of each census tract, each year, for years 2009-2023.







Table
Details




B25024
Units in Structure


B25001
Housing Units


B25003
Tenure (Owner Occupied vs Renter Occupied)


B25008
Total Population in Occupied Housing Units by Tenure


B25119
Median Household Income in the Past 12 Months (in 2024 Inflation-Adjusted Dollars) by Tenure


B25032
Tenure by Units in Structure


B25088
Median Selected Monthly Owner Costs (Dollars) by Mortgage Status


B19013
Median Household Income in the Past 12 Months (in 2024 Inflation-Adjusted Dollars)

title: Technical Report
text:
Model Selection and Validation
We built a two-way fixed effects (or fixed panel) regression model to identify within-tract drivers of ownership costs. We initially wanted to use a simple multiple linear regression model, but found that we were explaining almost none of the variance in our data. A fixed effects model helps to remedy the inevitable unknown elements of variance in such an economic model, as it holds certain elements constant to account for inherent variance.
Our model controls for tract-level fixed effects, meaning time-invariant characteristics of a neighborhood like location and county \(\times\) year fixed effects, which account for unknown variables like employer expansion and COVID-era impacts. Our model also excludes the years 2009-2013 to account for the economic recovery following the 2008 recession.
The model is as follows:
\[
y_{it} = \beta X_{it} + \alpha_i + \lambda_{c(i), t} + u_{i,t}
\]
where \(i =\) tract (Census GEOID), \(t =\) year, and \(c(i) =\) county of tract \(i\).
\(\alpha_i\) stands for tract fixed effect, which controls for anything constant within a tract over time, like location desirability, zoning baseline, and neighborhood quality. In other words, \(\alpha\) lets us compare a tract to itself over time. This means that all estimates are identified using within-tract changes over time and not differences between tracts.
\(\lambda_{c(i), t}\) stands for the county \(\times\) year fixed effect, controlling for anything affecting a county in a specific year; things like local economic shocks (ex: big employer entering or leaving), policy changes, or impacts from COVID. In other words, \(\lambda_{c(i), t}\) allows us to remove shared shocks across nearby areas at the same time.

title: Technical Report
text:
Model Building
Our model is implemented in Python and uses features of the statsmodels package as well as the linearmodels package.
We first filter the dataset to relevant variables, these being tract, county, year, monthly owner costs for households with a mortgage, percent of single-family homes that are renter-occupied, median household income, owner-renter income gap, percent of vacant single-family homes, and total population in tract. Note that we need each census tract to appear in at least two time periods to ensure we can identify the within-tract variation.
We next construct the regression design matrix. Fixed effects are incorporated using an absorbing approach rather than explicit dummy variables, which allows us to easily scale to thousands of tracts and county-year combinations. Two sets of fixed effects are included: tract fixed effects and county \(\times\) year fixed effects, which are constructed by interacting county and year into a single categorical variable. These absorbing effects are passed to the AbsorbingLS model as categorical variables, allowing the estimator to partial them out efficiently during estimation. The model is fit using clustered standard errors at the tract level.
In short, the estimation procedure follows these steps:

Prepare and clean the dataset


clean = _prepare(df, PREDICTORS)
clean["county_year"] = clean["county"].astype(str) + "_" + clean["year"].astype(str)


Construct predictor matrix \(X\) and outcome \(y\)


X = sm.add_constant(clean[PREDICTORS])


Define absorbed fixed effects (tract and county-year)


absorb = pd.DataFrame({
    "tract_fe":       pd.Categorical(clean["GEOID"]),
    "county_year_fe": pd.Categorical(clean["county_year"]),
})


Estimate the model via AbsorbingLS and compute cluster-robust standard errors at the tract level


model = AbsorbingLS(clean[OUTCOME], X, absorb=absorb)
result = model.fit(cov_type="clustered", clusters=pd.Categorical(clean["GEOID"]),)

title: Technical Report
text:
Analyses, Results, and Interpretation
A table depicting the results of the fixed panel model is below.

Fixed Effects Model (Tract FE + County×Year FE)

Observations: 8,284
Absorbed R²: 0.9455
The complexity of this model leads to a lot of things to consider. For example, while “Absorbed \(R^2\)” is impressively high, the county \(\times\) year fixed effect will by nature increase the \(R^2\) metric. So, we need to use other, more subjective metrics to judge the validity of our model. We found after fitting two different models (the first being a one-way fixed effects model) that main variables were more stable in our current model. Additionally, although not individually statistically significant, the other variables remain part of the joint explanatory structure of the model and contribute to overall fit.
In particular, the coefficients on median household income and the owner-renter income gap remained consistent in both magnitude and statistical significance, suggesting that these relationships are not driven by broader county-level shocks. Overall, this stability across model specifications provides evidence that our results are not sensitive to unobserved, time-varying county-level factors, and supports the use of the more complex model as a credible framework for identifying within-tract drivers of housing costs.

title: Technical Report
text:
Conclusions
We were successfully able to establish that significant contributors to the rise in house prices in Utah are median household income, the owner-renter income gap, and population growth, answering our original question of what factors are leading to a difficulty in purchasing homes in recent years.
While there are still many questions we would like to explore, like “how do zoning laws affect house prices?” and “is it really less profitable to build less expensive homes?”, we consider this project a success.
